content.full_text:
Re:
Fern Polrrrcel Pnecrrcrs CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o suite 620 o Sacramento, cA g5gl4-232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322_0886
November 16,2009
Bill Mabie
Chief of Staff
Office of Senator Alex Padilla
430 N. Cordova Street
Alhambra, CA 91801
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-09-231
Dear Mr. Mabie:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
and gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Aci).1 Bicause your question seeks
general guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair political practices
Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other applicable ethics laws or
rules outside the Act. In this regard, we suggest that you consuit with the Senate
committee on Legislative Ethics or the Legislative counsel Bureau.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government code Sections g1000 through gl014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through l8gg7 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
fbrmal written advice. (Section 831 l4; Regulation lgj29(cx3), copy enclosed.y


File No. I-09-231
Page No. 2
QIIESTTONS
1. Does the Act prohibit you from serving on the board of a nonprofit
organization, established and controlled by you and your wife that provides combat
veterans with tickets to various sporting events?
2. May you solicit tickets and donations for the nonprofit organization?
3. May you and your wife attend, and travel to and from, sporting events as
escorts for the selected veterans if the tickets and travel costs have been donated to the
nonprofit organization?
. 
CONCLUSIONS
l. While thd Act does not prohibit a public official from concurrently serving on
the board of a nonprofit organization, serving on the board may implicate thi Act's
conflict-of-interest provisions, which prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or influencing a governmental decision in which the official has a financiat
interests. (Section 87100.)
2. The Act does not prohibit a public official who serves on the board of a
nonprofit organization from soliciting donations to the organization. However, under the
facts you have provided, donations made to the nonprofit may constitute gifts to you that
are subject to disclosure and potentially prohibited.
3. Donations to your nonprofit organization, established and controlled by you
and your wife, used to pay for your attendance at sporting events will be consideied gifts
to you subject to the Act's reporting requirements and gilt limitations. Donations to the
organization used for your wife's attendance at sporting events will be considered gifts to
you, subject to the Act's reporting requirements and gift limitations, if you direct and
control the donations by soliciting the donations expressly for your wife's use.
FACTS
You are the Chief of Staff for.Senator Alex Padilla. You and your wife are
looking into creating a nonprofit organization. The nonprofit establisLed in the memory
of your father-in-law, a Vietnam Veteran, would provide free tickets to sporting events
for combat veterans. Your goal is to work with the Veterans Administration, aid others
who provide care for wounded veterans, to identify veterans who would most benefit
from the experience. Because of your past experience with nonprofit organizations, you
will be serving on the board of the organization. However, you will not be receiving a
salary for this position.


File No. I-09-231
Page No. 3
If permitted under the Act, you would like to be involved in soliciting tickets and
donations for the organization. Solicitations would be made to individuals and
coFpanies who have season tickets as well as the event providers themselves. In
addition, you expect, particularly during the start up of the organization, that you and./or
your wife may attend, and travel to and from, the events as escorts for the selected
veterans.
ANALYSN
l. May you serve on the boanl of a nonprofit organization established and controlled by
you and your wife?
The Act does not prohibit a public official from concurrently serving on the board
of a nonprofit organization. However, other bodies of law or rules applicable to Senate
employees may restrict such activities. As we do not offer advice beyond the confines of
the Act, we encourage you to consult with the Senate Committee on Legislative Ethics or
the Legislative Counsel Bureau to ensure that your activities do not vioLte laws or rules
outside the Act.
Nonetheless, receiving income or gifts for serving on the board of a nonprofit
organization while concurrently serving as a state employee may implicate the Act,s
conflict-of-interest provisions, which prohibit a publii oifi.ial fiom making, participating
in making, or influencing a govemmental decision in which the official has-a financial
interest. (Section 87100.) A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable thalthe decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of thi public official,s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-
step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of
interest in a given governmental decision.
Since you offer no facts at this point that would enable us to provide a complete
analysis of whether you have a conflict of interest under the Act in any particular
decision, we will not do so here. However, if you receive payments lincluding free
tickets) from the organization, other than payments for the reimbursement of tiavel
expenses and per diem from a bona fide nonprofit entity exempt from taxation under
Section 501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue Code, they may conititute income or gifts from
the organization that may potentially disqualify you from making, participating in
making, or influencing governmental decisions affecting the organization. (Sition
82030.) Moreover, under the facts you have provided, you *uy also have economic
interests in sources of donations including travel costs to the nonprofit organization, as


File No. l-09-231
Page No.4
analyzed below, that may potentially disqualify you from making, participating in
making, or influencing governmental decisions affecting the souices.3
2. May you solicit donations to the organization?
The Act does not prohibit a public official, serving on the board of a nonprofit
organization, from soliciting donations to the organization. However, under theiacts you
have provided, donations made to the nonprofit, as analyzed below, may constitute gifts
to you that are subject to disclosure and potentially p.ohibit"d.  
'
3. May you or your wife attend, and travel to andfrom, sporting events as an escott for
veterans selected by the organization to attend the events if the tickets and travel costs
have been donated to the organization?
The term "gift" is defined in Section 8202g(a) as:
"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to
the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the
rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to members of
the public without regard to official status.',S
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the
receipt of gifts by public officials in three ways that are relevant to your questio-n.6
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public
officials. The current limit is $420 or more from a single so*c" in a calendur y"ur.
(Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) Under Section 89503(c), this gift limit applies to
any designated employee of a state or local governmental agency if the membei or
employee, such as you, would be required to disclose the reieipi of income or gifts from
the source of the gift on his or her statement of Economic lnterests.
3 Ifyou have additional questions regarding your participation in any particular decision
potentially affecting the nonprofit organization or a source ofdonations to the nonprofit organization, it is
advisable that you seek further advice providing all relevant facts related to the deiision.
o Note that as a nonelected state employee donations you solicit for the nonprofit organization are
not behested paymenrs subject ro reporting under Section g2015(bX2)(BXiii).
5 Section 82o44 d.efines payment, in part, as any "rendering of . . . services or anything else of
value, whether tangible or intangible."
6 The Act also prohibits certain state officials, including legislative otficials such as you, from
accepting gifts from, or arranged by, a registered state lobbyist in excess of $ l0 in a calendar month. lSee
Sections 86201, 86203, and 86204.) However, since you do not indicate that a lobbyist is involved in
making the payments in question, we do not analyze this issue.


File No. l-09-231
Page No. 5
Secondly, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts,
the Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public ofliciut, requiring that any gift 1o.
any gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received diring the
calendar year are disclosed on the officials' Statements of Economic Interests. (Sections
87200,87203,87207,87300, andB730Z.) Under Section g7302(b), these reporting
obligations apply to employees, such as you, designated in an agency,s confl-ict-of-
interest code as specified in the code.
Finally, as analyzed above, the Act prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or influencing the outcome of a gor"*mental decision involving
the donor of a gift o1 sifls with an aggregate value of $42b or more provided to, received
by, or promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date of the decision.
(Sections 87100, 87103(e); Regulations 1g700, 19703.4.)
Generally, the Act does not treat persons working for or serving on the boards of
nonprofit entities as recipients of donations received by the entities. Fir example, in
previously examining the question of whether donations to a nonprofit organirution
would be treated as gifts to a Senator if the Senator requested the donatiois and was the
president of the organization, the Commission made tht foilowing determination:
"The reporting requirernent and [gift] prohibition...apply only to
gifts that are made to the legislator and clearly are not applicaLt! to tle
instant situation.
"As president of the contra costa youth Association, senator
Nejedly makes appeals for money, goods and services to be utilized by the
Association. These necessities are given to the Association, not Senator
Nejedly. Accordingly, the donations generated by the Senator's
solicitations are gifts to the Association, not to the Senator J, (In re
Nejedly (1976) 2 FppC Ops.46.)
More importantly to this analysis, the Commission, however, cautioned that the
result would be different if the organization merely acted as an agent for the Senator.
Similarly, in the O'Shea Advice Letter, No. I-90-593, we proviaJA tne following caveat
in concluding that donations to a nonprofit civic league would not be treated u, iif,, ,o
public officials who were members of the league's board of directors:
"Care should be taken to assure that donations to the agency are
not deemed to be donations to individual board members. This could
occur, for example, if donations were used to pay travel expenses for
board members. However, so long as donations are not deemed to be
donations to members, the donors do not become economic interests of
those board members."


File No. I-09-231
Page No.6
Under the facts you have provided, you and your wife would like to establish and
control a nonprofit organization whose primary, and perhaps sole, purpose is to provide
veterans with the opportunity to attend various sporting events. To achieve this goal, you
plan to organize the organization so that most, if not all, donations to the organization
will be tickets or payments for admission or travel to sporting events. Given this
circumstance, donations to your nonprofit organization used ior you. attendance at
sporting events are payments conferring a personal benefit upon you for which the donor
has not received equal or greater consideration. Notwithstanding even your best
intentions, il also appeTs that the organization will be acting as your agent in accepting
donations to your benefit considering the fact that you and your wife will control the
newly established organization. Accordingly, donations to the nonprofit organization
used to pay for your attendance at sporting events, including ticketi and traiel payments,
will be considered gifts to you subject to the Act's reporting requirements and gift
limitations.
Tuming to donations to the nonprofit organization used for your wife's attendance
at sporting events, Regulation 18944 provides the Commission,s standards for
determining when a gift to an official's family is a gift to the official and provides in
pertinent part:
"(b) A gift given to a member or members of an official's
immediate family is not a gift to the official unless it confers a personal
benefit on the official. A gift given to a member or member. oi un
official's immediate family confers a 'personal benefit, on the official for
purposes of this regulation, when any of the following factors apply:
"(l) Benefit: The official enjoys direct benefit from the gift, except
for a benefit of nominal value;
"(2) Use: The official uses the gift, and the official's use is not
nominal or incidental to the use by the member or members of the
official's immediate family;
"(3) Discretion and Control: The official exercises discretion and
control over who will use the gift or dispose of the gift."
If you solicit the donations expressly for your wife's use, the donations to the
organization will be considered gifts to you subject to the Act's reporting requirements
and gift limitations because you will have exerted discretion and ctntrol ovei the gifts.
On the other hand, donations to the nonprofit organization used for your wife,s
attendance at sporting events will be considered gifts to your wife, and not gifts to you
subject to reporting and gift limitations, so long as you do not solicit the donations
expressly for your wife's use.


File No. l-09-231
Page No. 7
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, I*gal Divis ion
Enclosure

control the donations by soliciting the donations expressly for your wife's use.
